label: SpaceX HQ — Hawthorne, CA
sub: SpaceX's primary corporate, engineering, and manufacturing campus at 1 Rocket Road. Houses Falcon 9/Heavy and Dragon production lines, mission control, and executive operations in a 550,000 sq ft former Boeing factory.

label: Starbase — Boca Chica, TX
sub: SpaceX's Starship/Super Heavy development and launch complex. Home of the Orbital Launch Mount, Mechazilla catch tower, and all Starship integrated flight tests. Incorporated as the City of Starbase in 2025.

label: Launch Complex 39A — KSC, FL
sub: Historic Apollo and Space Shuttle pad leased by SpaceX from NASA since 2014. Primary east-coast pad for Falcon Heavy and crewed Crew Dragon missions. First SpaceX launch from LC-39A was February 19, 2017.

label: SLC-40 — Cape Canaveral, FL
sub: SpaceX's highest-cadence Falcon 9 launch pad, leased since 2007. Handles the majority of Starlink, commercial, and cargo missions. Destroyed in the 2016 AMOS-6 explosion; rebuilt and returned to service in 2017.

label: SLC-4E — Vandenberg SFB, CA
sub: SpaceX's west-coast Falcon 9 launch pad at Vandenberg Space Force Base, used for polar and sun-synchronous orbit missions. Supports heavy Starlink polar-orbit constellation launches. First SpaceX launch: September 29, 2013.

label: Landing Zone 1 (LZ-1) — Cape Canaveral, FL
sub: Built on former Launch Complex 13. Site of the first-ever orbital rocket booster landing on December 21, 2015 (ORBCOMM OG2 mission). Primary east-coast Return-to-Launch-Site landing pad for Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy.

label: Landing Zone 4 (LZ-4) — Vandenberg, CA
sub: West-coast RTLS booster landing pad built on former SLC-4W launch site, approximately 300 meters from SLC-4E. First landing: October 8, 2018 (SAOCOM 1A mission). Supports polar orbit Falcon 9 missions.

label: Rocket Test Facility — McGregor, TX
sub: SpaceX's primary rocket engine testing facility on a 300-acre site (former Bluebonnet Ordnance Plant). Every Merlin, Raptor, and Draco engine is hot-fire tested here before flight. Grasshopper VTVL tests were also conducted here.

label: SpaceX Redmond — Starlink Dev, WA
sub: SpaceX's satellite R&D and manufacturing center established in 2015. Houses Starlink constellation design, satellite production, and orbit-control operations. Center of the Gen2 Starlink satellite development program.

label: ASDS 'OCISLY' — Pacific Recovery Zone
sub: Approximate deployment zone for drone ship 'Of Course I Still Love You' supporting Falcon 9 booster recovery for Vandenberg launches (since June 2021). Home-ported in Long Beach, CA. Deploys 600-1,200+ km downrange depending on mission profile.

label: ASDS 'JRTI' — Atlantic Recovery Zone
sub: Approximate deployment zone for drone ship 'Just Read the Instructions' supporting Falcon 9/Heavy recovery for KSC/Cape Canaveral launches. Home-ported at Port Canaveral. Typically deploys ~300 km northeast into the Atlantic.

label: Landing Zone 2 (LZ-2) — Cape Canaveral, FL
sub: Secondary east-coast RTLS pad adjacent to LZ-1. Used for Falcon Heavy side-booster simultaneous twin landings (first use: Falcon Heavy test flight, February 6, 2018). Both LZ-1 and LZ-2 are at the former Launch Complex 13.

label: SpaceX Launch Control — Cape Canaveral, FL
sub: SpaceX's east-coast Launch and Landing Control Center, managing all Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and Dragon missions from Kennedy Space Center and Cape Canaveral. Houses vehicle processing and integration facilities.

label: Port Canaveral — Dragon Recovery Port
sub: Home port for SpaceX's recovery fleet including the drone ship 'Just Read the Instructions' and crew recovery vessels. Dragon Crew capsules perform Atlantic splashdowns near the Florida coast and are recovered by SpaceX ships.

label: Starship Factory — Starbase, TX
sub: SpaceX's dedicated Starship and Super Heavy manufacturing facility at Boca Chica. Produces Raptor engines, stainless steel tank sections, and assembles full Starship vehicles on site adjacent to the launch complex.

label: SpaceX Launch Complex — KSC Horizontal Integration
sub: SpaceX's Dragon spacecraft and Falcon 9 upper stage horizontal integration facility at KSC, adjacent to the VAB. Crew Dragon capsules are processed and integrated here before being mated to Falcon 9 for crewed ISS missions.

label: Omelek Island — Kwajalein Atoll (Falcon 1 Site)
sub: Remote Pacific island in the Marshall Islands where SpaceX conducted all five Falcon 1 launches (2006-2009). The site was leased from the US Army's Reagan Test Site. All four early launches and one success occurred here before SpaceX transitioned to Falcon 9 at Cape Canaveral.

label: Former Launch Complex 13 — LZ-1/LZ-2 Site
sub: The original Atlas ICBM launch site, decommissioned in 1978, was transformed by SpaceX into Landing Zones 1 and 2. The site's existing concrete infrastructure made it an ideal landing pad. Historic: first orbital rocket booster landing December 21, 2015.

label: Starlink Network Operations — Seattle, WA
sub: SpaceX's Starlink satellite network operations center managing constellation positioning, collision avoidance, and broadband service delivery. The facility coordinates real-time control of over 10,000 satellites and user terminal software updates.

label: Vandenberg Space Launch Complex 6 (SLC-6)
sub: Historic Space Shuttle West Coast launch facility at Vandenberg, now associated with SpaceX operations in the broader Vandenberg launch corridor. SpaceX's SLC-4E and LZ-4 operations have rejuvenated west-coast launch activity.

label: Boca Chica Village — Starbase Community
sub: Small Texas coastal community adjacent to Starbase that SpaceX purchased extensively for buffer zones and worker housing. The village's residents were bought out over years; the area is now part of the incorporated City of Starbase. A model for private company-owned launch communities.

label: Johnson Space Center — Houston, TX
sub: NASA's human spaceflight operations headquarters, home to Mission Control for ISS and Commercial Crew operations. SpaceX Crew Dragon missions are controlled from the Christopher C. Kraft Jr. Mission Control Center here alongside SpaceX's own mission control in Hawthorne.

label: Vehicle Assembly Building — KSC, FL
sub: NASA's iconic VAB has been used by SpaceX for Falcon Heavy stacking and is planned for future Starship processing. The 160-meter tall building remains one of the world's largest by volume and is central to SpaceX's east-coast heavy-lift operations.

label: Cape Canaveral Space Force Station — HQ
sub: The USSF installation hosting SpaceX's SLC-40 launch pad and LZ-1/LZ-2 landing zones. Cape Canaveral has been SpaceX's primary east-coast launch base since 2010. The co-location of launch and landing infrastructure at the same base is a key feature enabling rapid turnaround.

label: SpaceX Starlink Facility — Irvine, CA
sub: SpaceX's secondary Starlink development and testing facility in Southern California, handling antenna design, user terminal hardware development, and ground station infrastructure for the global broadband constellation.

label: Gulf of Mexico — Crew Dragon Splashdown Zone
sub: The northern Gulf of Mexico has been the standard splashdown zone for Crew Dragon missions returning from the ISS (beginning with Demo-2 in August 2020). The location offers proximity to Pensacola and Panama City recovery infrastructure while avoiding Atlantic hurricane corridors.

label: Crew Dragon Integration — Hawthorne, CA
sub: SpaceX's Crew Dragon production, assembly, and SuperDraco abort engine testing operations at the Hawthorne campus. All Crew Dragon capsules are manufactured here before being transported to Kennedy Space Center for mission preparation.

label: Brownsville, TX — Regional Aerospace Hub
sub: The city closest to Starbase, serving as the regional economic hub for SpaceX operations in South Texas. The SpaceX presence has transformed Brownsville's local economy and infrastructure, with the SpaceX workforce driving real estate and service sector growth since 2014.

label: SpaceX Cocoa — Florida Operations Hub
sub: SpaceX's Cocoa, Florida facility serves as an operations and logistics hub for Cape Canaveral launches, supporting booster refurbishment, payload processing, and launch campaign operations. The facility handles Falcon 9 first-stage inspections and component logistics between missions.

label: Pacific Dragon Splashdown Zone — Pacific Coast
sub: The Pacific Ocean off the California coast has been used for Dragon capsule water recoveries for Vandenberg-origin missions. The splashdown zone is approximately 400-600 km west of Vandenberg, with recovery ships deploying from Port Hueneme or nearby ports.
